summary: This lesson covers how Spanish avoids possessive adjectives with body parts (using 'me duele la pierna' instead of 'mi pierna duele'), the use of 'de' for possession (la casa de José), and introduces 'solo' (only/just) and 'dolor' (pain).

teachings.summary: In Spanish, you don't say 'my leg hurts' (mi pierna duele). Instead you say 'it hurts me the leg' (me duele la pierna). Spanish uses indirect object pronouns with body parts rather than possessive adjectives.
teachings.rule: For body part pain: [indirect object pronoun] + duele/duelen + la/el [body part]. NOT mi/tu/su + [body part] + duele.

examples.english: My leg hurts. (It hurts me the leg.)

examples.spanish: No me duele la pierna más.

teachings.notes: Doler has an o-split: duele (not 'dole'). This is part of a broader pattern where Spanish uses fewer possessive adjectives than English.

teachings.summary: Doler works like gustar -- it takes an indirect object pronoun. The o splits to ue when stressed (duele). The noun form is dolor (pain).

examples.english: I have a headache. (pain of head)

teachings.summary: Spanish expresses possession with 'de' (of) rather than the English 's. 'José's house' becomes 'la casa de José' (the house of José).
teachings.rule: [article] + [noun] + de + [owner] for possession. Like 'dolor de cabeza' (ache of the head).

examples.spanish: Vamos a la casa de José.
examples.english: We are going to José's house.

examples.spanish: Vamos a la fiesta de María.
examples.english: We are going to Maria's party.

teachings.summary: Solo means 'only' or 'just' in Spanish. It can be placed before or after the verb, with different emphasis.

examples.english: I spoke with you only. (more emphatic)

examples.spanish: No hablé con nadie más en la fiesta de María, solo hablé contigo.
examples.english: I didn't speak with anyone else at Maria's party, I only spoke with you.
